w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Corrected the issue with the primary and secondary value address table
Corrected the issue with the decoder incorrectly recognizing CS
Please note that some primary and secondary value addresses have been swapped.
Simplify the information, update the software configuration instructions, and update the decoder
3.5.1 Set the data to be uploaded
abel on the front of the meter
data has a default template, and the
onnect meter to a computer using an RS485 to USB converter.
The + sign on meter is connected to 21 port , and the – sign on meter is connected to 22 port.
ADW300 configuration software EN T version 1.1 and above are recommended
number is read out for reference only when engineers debug, where the number has nothing to do with the actual operating frequency band of the module.
hey have an initial value at the factory, usually
A random APPKEY value can be obtained
The time interval between data uploads
, can be change to second (at least 5 sec).
They can all be set to the desired number.
Read the data before setting it.

is DR4. Due to the need to upload fixed length packets, ADR is disabled in the module, so we need to set a data transfer rate level that the module can recognize, which is DataRate. After setting, the corresponding spread spectrum factor is fixed,
and the maximum length of data that can be sent per packet is fixed.
The maximum data load (unit: byte) of a single packet for each DataRate in each frequency band

The larger the DataRate Number, the smaller the spread factor and the shorter the transmission time, the more data information is transmitted per unit time, but the anti-interference ability is reduced, so the transmission distance is shortened.

On the contrary, you can improve the anti-interference of the transmission by setting a smaller DataRate, and extend the transmission distance, and he packet may be divided into more packets to be sent, depending on the corresponding single packet maximum load.
The maximum DataRate Level corresponding to the US915 is DR3
In general, the number of channels is set to 0, which means that the module will run on default channels within the
Set the corresponding channel frequencies and the number of channels as needed. By default, for channels 0-7, the reading will not display the default values
Click Read to view the data packet mode currently in use.
In this mode, there is a default data template
available for upload. It can also be customized.
Special template, upload data is fixed. In this mode, updata settings cannot be modified.

This is the setting of what data the meter uploads. You can select the required data fields according to the complete address table, all the start address and register length can be found in the complete address table.
We can choose to upload the secondary value or the primary value(real value)
. The following is a description of the Updata Settings.
Set the data to be uploaded
When we want to upload the data include PT

Temperature of N phase. We first find the position of the parameter in the data table, like this PT, whose start address is 14. The next one is CT, start address is 15. And then Temperature of N phase, start address is 16.
You can see that after the name of the parameter in the table is the number of bytes, this value divided by 2 is the size of the register occupied by the parameter.

So when we set a group of the data, we fill in the start address first, the address is 14. And then the register length, which is 3. So that the three parameters selected will be added to the package in order.
Select parameters that are not adjacent
A group of data must be in adjacent states. When you need non-adjacent data, you can set it in different groups.
When we want the data include UA
IC. But they are not adjacent to the n-phase temperature parameter, so we need to set it to the new group.
So we find the start address is 20, Each data account for 1 register, so there are 9 registers in total. Then we set like :
Selects data that occupies two registers

When we need more parameters such as Active power of A phase, it may be involved that some data is 4 bytes in the address table, that is, 2 registers, which is twice the size of the normal data. We just need to calculate the total number of registers that this set of data actually occupies.

As shown in the table, we need these data, some data account for 4 bytes, and some data account for 2 bytes, converted to the number of registers, the sum can be 29 registers. So we fill in the start address and the number of registers as follows:
Num is the number of valid data groups. There are a total of 20 groups that can be filled in, and after filling in the required data group, fill in the rest of the 0.
Finally set the total number of groups. Like the picture, there are 9 groups of data, so set

If the num value is less than the total number of data groups you entered, for example, we entered 9 groups here, but if num only enter 1, only the first set of data will be uploaded in the data packet. The packet only includes PT
Temperature of N phase. No other data will be uploaded.

The byte size or number of registers that data represents in the data address table does not represent the byte length that data will occupy when it is finally uploaded. Whether the selected data is originally 2 bytes or 4 bytes, it is unified to 5 bytes per parameter (1 sequence number +4 bytes of data) when uploaded.
Enable to add a CS checksum byte to the end of the uploaded data packet.
This option is selected by default for the initial template
Checksum: All the data in the uploaded packet is added to obtain a value, and then the value is divided by 256 to obtain a remainder, which is the CS checksum.
For example, a packet contains this data:
If we need CS, then the CS checksum is added to the end:
So in the absence of CS, the packet size is a multiple of 5. If we add CS it's a multiple of 5, plus 1 byte.
The function of CS checksum calculation is constructed by C language
// start is the location of the first data.
// n is the number of bytes computed.
uint8_t CS16(uint8_t* pBus, uint16_t start, uint16_t n)
Select whether we want big endian.
This option is selected by default for the initial template.
If we upload a parameter with a value of 0x12345678, then in the big endian condition it looks like this:
n the small endian condition it looks like this:

After this function is enabled, the data upload frequency changes from minute to second. For example, the original data frequency was 5, and the data was uploaded every 5 minutes. After the second level upload was enabled, the data was uploaded every 5 seconds.
It is not selected by default.
If you need to use it, consult engineers first.
By default, or by selection, the packet type is Selectable Data Packet. In this mode, there is an initial template, and we can also customize it.
The initial template contains a total of 9 data groups, including 68 parameters and 1 CS checksum. The data is in big endian.
All parameters have 4 bytes of space in the packet, but the
data type may be int16, uint16, int32, uint32, float
. What is the specific type of the value of each parameter according to the data list or address table.
at the last of all data.
Notice : Mostly data are secondary side values from the meter. Need to deal data for getting real value.
The following table lists the data for the initial template. And the table explains how to process the
If the reading value is 105, the temperature is
Total energy consumption on A phase
Forward active energy consumption on A phase
Reversing active energy consumption on A phase
Forward reactive energy consumption on A phase
Reversing reactive energy consumption on A phase
Total energy consumption on B phase
Forward active energy consumption on B phase
Reversing active energy consumption on B phase
Forward reactive energy consumption on B phase
Reversing reactive energy consumption on B phase
Total energy consumption on C phase
Forward active energy consumption on C phase
Reversing active energy consumption on C phase
Forward reactive energy consumption on C phase
Reversing reactive energy consumption on C phase
Maximum forward active demand in current month
Total distortion rate of voltage and current on each phase
In this mode, there is a special data template and it cannot be changed.
It is a retention of the old version template
This will not be elaborated here
If you want to freely define the content of the uploaded data, you can select the data groups from the following tables.
The first column is the start address in hexadecimal.
The second column is the start address in decimal.
The third column is variable name.
The fourth column is data length (divide by 2 to get the number of register stored).
The fifth column indicates whether the data is readable or writable.
The sixth column describes the data type, decimal cases, and how to deal the data.
High byte: parity mode, low byte: stop Bit
High byte: 0-none, 1-even, 2-odd; low byte: 0- 1 stop Bit, 1- 2 stop Bit
If the reading value is 105, the temperature is 10.5℃
Time, date (second, minute, hour, day, month, year)
Total energy consumption on A phase
Forward active energy consumption on A phase
Reversing active energy consumption on A phase
Forward reactive energy consumption on A phase
Reversing reactive energy consumption on A phase
Total energy consumption on B phase
Forward active energy consumption on B phase
Reversing active energy consumption on B phase
Forward reactive energy consumption on B phase
Reversing reactive energy consumption on B phase
Total energy consumption on C phase
Forward active energy consumption on C phase
Reversing active energy consumption on C phase
Forward reactive energy consumption on C phase
Reversing reactive energy consumption on C phase
Maximum forward active demand in current month
Maximum reversing active demand in current month
Maximum forward reactive demand in current month
Maximum reversing reactive demand in current month
Total distortion rate of voltage and current on each phase
Fundamental active power on A phase
Fundamental active power on B phase
Fundamental active power on C phase
Fundamental reactive power on A phase
Fundamental reactive power on B phase
Fundamental reactive power on C phase
Harmonic active power on A phase
Harmonic active power on B phase
Harmonic active power on C phase
Harmonic reactive power on A phase
Harmonic reactive power on B phase
Harmonic reactive power on C phase
Time zone number/Time zone date: day
Time zone date: month/Time zone number
Time zone date: day/ Time zone date: month
Time zone number/Time zone date: day
Time zone date: month/Time zone number
Time zone date: day/ Time zone date: month
1-14 period of time Parameters setting information

Current first quadrant of reactive energy
